item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I never thought I would perceive a train as being cute. Well done with this skip.
+1

item.title: Staff Post - Image Licensing
item.content:encoded: The original image has been sourced to a product photo on a toy sale website, and there is no known CC source I can find for it and the author has not provided one. As such, the image has been removed. Author, if you replace the image with a future one, please ensure that is complies with our image use policy and make sure to contact a member of licensing team for approval after putting it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well that is just adorable, a great example of why SCPs don't have to be horror all the time!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was cute. It made me happy.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I genuinely quite loved this.

It absolutely has its issues but there's so much here that I enjoy. The dejection when its gifts are refused, its behavior when locked in a room, the poor thing's reaction to being yelled at :[

There are a few issues, though, I think you should think about. First one is:

There is currently no plan to capture SCP-4719, and it is doubtful there will ever be one.

Lines like this crop up in a lot of SCP drafts I've seen. It's not the sort of thing that would be said as rarely do procedures speculate about the future like this. This reads more like an opinion than a fact.

Also, the acquisition. I am not sure if I'm missing a reference or if you're just intentionally being cagey here but I don't get much from it. There's not enough information there even for my imagination to run wild. And since the redacted SCP is not given any information, really, I'm just not sure what I'm supposed to take from it.

A human kidney

I don't think the Foundation would let the transplant happen. The Kidney just got anomalously created out of thin air. Who knows what might be wrong with it. I imagine the fellow would still get a transplant, but I don't think it would be from this specific kidney.

There may be a bit more, but this really was great. Thanks for the super charming read! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suspect the fact that it doesn't want to leave the site is enough to make it de facto contained, especially if you define "contained" as "in accordance with the item's listed containment procedures".

Also, not sure something can be Keter just by virtue of being uncontained or even uncontainable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it's uncontained, shouldn't it be either Uncontained or Keter, not Euclid? Otherwise, it's pretty cute. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reading this I was expecting it to end up being like SCP-1048, but I like that you subverted that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I might make a few possible suggestions for resolving the "never leaves the site" issue?

  • There is another object that the SCP is known to stick around.
  • It is suspected that the origins of 4719 are in some way related to one of the properties Site-152 was built on former status as an orphanage or a children's hospital.
  • Dr. Wondertainment did it.

If I were writing this, my solution would be the first, but the second also suggests the "where did it come from" angle.

Sorry, didn't notice the first "Acquisition" paragraph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first two paragraphs of the Description contain non-metric measurements - that's an easy fix.

Like Cyantreuse, the story here didn't grab me. The teleportation effect seems entirely tacked on to justify the lack of containment, and it's never made clear why the train never leaves the site if it's not contained. The carriages and gifts sections didn't really pique my interest sufficiently to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed on switching behavior and table. Otherwise, I really enjoyed this one. It's cute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this concept for being cute, but my issues with the execution prevent me from upvoting. The article starts to feel reiterative quickly. A lot of the "Behavior" section (not sure why that's separate and not in the Description, as it's still describing the anomaly) regarding the gifts was stuff already inferred from the table; I wonder if reversing the positions of the table and "behavior" description could help, though there's still just going to be extra text. There are a lot of paragraphs here and the anomaly itself could easily be described in three or four, IMO, just based on the information scattered throughout long-winded phrasing. For instance, the entire first paragraph of "Acquisition" doesn't add any backstory; all we learn is that some other SCP caused it. The last paragraph of that section is stuff already gone over, mostly in the ConProcs, so the Final Ending Line falls flat.

but politely—and perhaps wisely—refused to accept. After about 5 minutes of pestering her, SCP-4719 finally gave up and went away.

While this was the most glaring example, it's not the only section of iffy tone. I see that you were intentionally going for a Series I feel, but I think this does that fine by concept alone, and could do without elements like quote-marked "cries" or "whines" — it's still not clinical language, even if it's a hallmark of older articles.

item.title: Author's Notes
item.content:encoded:
Wrote this thing for the Cliché-Con 2019 doohickey.

Category 1 — An anomaly so helpful/harmless, the Foundation lets it roam the Site and has minimal containment procedures.

I tried to write something more cutesy here and reminiscent of earlier Series articles. I'm hoping to use this as the beginning for a set of articles centered around the yet-written "SCP-████" mentioned in this article, and a bit of shenanigans around Site-152. Hope you enjoy!
